questions.q: To plan a route-finding app, a developer represents a city's roads as a graph of nodes (junctions) and weighted edges (road lengths), ignoring road surface, colour and width. Which thinking skill is this?
questions.explain: Removing irrelevant detail (surface, colour, width) to leave only what matters for the problem is thinking abstractly. Modelling the roads as a graph is representational abstraction.

questions.q: Before writing a binary search, a programmer notes that the list MUST already be sorted for the search to work correctly. Identifying this requirement is an example of:
questions.explain: A precondition is a condition that must be true before a routine runs correctly, such as the list being sorted before a binary search. Recognising it is part of thinking ahead (2.1.2).

questions.q: A developer designs an online shop as separate sub-procedures: addToBasket, then calculateTotal, then takePayment, then dispatchOrder, where each step depends on the output of the one before. This is mainly:
questions.explain: Breaking a problem into an ordered sequence of sub-procedures, where the order matters because steps depend on earlier outputs, is thinking procedurally (decomposition).

questions.q: In a game, the code must decide what happens next using the test IF score >= highScore THEN update record ELSE continue. Spotting and defining this branch point is:
questions.explain: Identifying a decision point where the flow of control branches on a Boolean condition, with both branches defined, is thinking logically (2.1.4).

questions.q: A weather app must download data from three independent regional servers. Since none depends on the others, the developer fetches all three at the same time to finish sooner. This is an example of:
questions.explain: Spotting independent tasks (no data dependency) that can run at the same time to improve throughput is thinking concurrently (2.1.5).

questions.q: A team builds a generalised Account class so that both savings accounts and current accounts can reuse it. Which form of abstraction is this?
questions.explain: Grouping many things by their shared characteristics into one general, reusable model (Account for savings and current) is generalisation/categorisation abstraction, as opposed to representational abstraction of one specific thing.

questions.q: A web app repeatedly performs an expensive currency-conversion calculation, so the developer stores recent results in fast memory to avoid recomputing them. This technique is:
questions.explain: Storing expensive or frequently used results in fast storage for reuse is caching, part of thinking ahead. It speeds things up but uses extra storage and risks stale data needing invalidation.

questions.q: A developer reuses a pre-tested, self-contained date-validation module across several projects instead of rewriting it each time. Which benefit of reusable components does this BEST illustrate?
options: Faster development with fewer bugs because the module is already tested
It removes the need for any preconditions
questions.explain: Reusable components are generalised, self-contained modules. Because they are already tested, they speed up development, reduce bugs, and give consistency and easier maintenance.

questions.q: Designing a payroll routine, a developer lists the employee ID and hours worked as data going IN, and the net pay figure as the result coming OUT. Identifying these is part of:
questions.explain: Determining the inputs (data in) and outputs (results out) before coding is part of thinking ahead. Note that 'validate the data' would be a process, not an input or output.

questions.q: Two concurrent threads both try to update the same shared bank balance, and the final value depends on the unpredictable order in which they run. What problem is this?
questions.explain: A race condition occurs when the result depends on the unpredictable timing of concurrent tasks that share data. It is a key trade-off of thinking concurrently.

questions.q: A student writes that they 'broke the problem into smaller sub-problems'. Which thinking skill have they actually described, and why is it NOT abstraction?
options: Decomposition — abstraction removes/hides detail rather than splitting the problem
Abstraction — they are the same thing
Concurrency — because sub-problems run in parallel
Logical thinking — because each part needs a condition
questions.explain: Splitting a problem into smaller sub-problems is decomposition. Abstraction instead removes or hides unnecessary detail to simplify; the two are different skills and must not be blurred.

questions.q: A booking system must allow a seat to be reserved only when enough seats remain. Which expresses this decision point most precisely as required by the spec?
options: If there are enough seats then book
IF seatsRequested <= seatsAvailable THEN reserve ELSE reject
questions.explain: Thinking logically requires an exact Boolean condition with both branches defined. 'IF seatsRequested <= seatsAvailable THEN reserve ELSE reject' is precise, unlike the vague natural-language versions.

questions.q: A developer claims a task will definitely run faster if made concurrent. Which point most correctly challenges that claim?
options: On a single core it is time-slicing, not true parallelism, and overhead plus dependencies can prevent any speed-up
Concurrency removes the need for synchronisation
Concurrency only ever helps with input and output
questions.explain: Concurrency is not automatically faster: on a single core it is time-slicing rather than true parallelism, there is scheduling/synchronisation overhead, and inherently sequential or data-dependent tasks cannot be parallelised.

questions.q: A cache holds a product's price, but the price has since changed in the database, so the cached value is now out of date. The cached value is described as:
questions.explain: Stale data is cached data that is out of date because the source has changed. It is the key trade-off of caching and is why caches need invalidation.
